source.title: Beta Oscillations in Working Memory, Executive Control of Movement and Thought, and Sensorimotor Function

confidence_rationale: This review establishes that beta oscillations serve as a 'status quo' maintenance signal: elevated beta during posture hold and post-movement periods suppresses alternative motor plans, while beta desynchronization permits movement initiation. Directly supports MECH-057a's claim that committed sequences suppress precision updates. High-quality review with strong mechanistic detail.
failure_signatures: The review notes that beta's role extends beyond simple suppression -- it also reflects working memory maintenance, content reactivation, and top-down predictions. MECH-057a's framing as pure precision suppression may be too narrow.
Beta suppression during movement may reflect preparation rather than precision updating -- the timing is pre-movement, not post-commitment.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Beta oscillations in sensorimotor cortex maintain the current motor state and suppress alternative actions. Elevated beta during posture hold and post-movement periods reflects 'status quo' maintenance. Beta desynchronization before movement permits action initiation. This framework extends to cognitive domains: beta may maintain working memory content and suppress interference.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-057a claims that committed action sequences suppress precision updates until execution completes. The beta status-quo hypothesis provides the neural mechanism: elevated beta during committed execution suppresses new sensory/precision inputs (analogous to E3 precision updates), while completion events (beta desynchronization, PMBR) re-open the update channel. MECH-090's beta-gate proposal implements this computationally.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The review discusses beta in the context of individual movements and posture, not multi-step action sequences per se. MECH-057a addresses committed multi-step sequences, which involves a longer timescale of suppression. Whether beta maintains its suppressive role across an extended action sequence (rather than just during a single movement) is less well established.
mapping.source_context: Review of beta oscillation literature across sensorimotor, cognitive, and clinical domains in humans and primates

confidence_components.notes: J Neuroscience review, comprehensive and well-cited. Mapping fidelity moderate-good because the status-quo hypothesis aligns closely with MECH-057a but the temporal scale (single movement vs multi-step sequence) differs. Transfer risk low.
